disclaimer: Claude's Picks are an AI's reasoned opinion for educational interest — NOT financial advice, a recommendation, or a guarantee. 10-year projections are illustrative estimates and will be wrong in unknowable ways. Do your own research.

growth.name: NVIDIA
growth.risks: AI-capex digestion or a hyperscaler over-build correction hitting an already-elevated valuation.
growth.rationale: The platform standard for AI compute, with a CUDA software moat 18+ years deep and full-stack lock-in (GPU + NVLink + networking + software). Data-center demand is broadening beyond the big hyperscalers to AI clouds, enterprises and sovereigns, and the annual roadmap cadence extends the lead just as inference scales. NVIDIA captures the highest-margin layer of the largest capex category in tech history.

growth.name: Microsoft
growth.risks: Massive AI capex compressing returns if Copilot/AI monetization disappoints.
growth.rationale: The closest thing to an index on enterprise software, cloud and AI, with three reinforcing flywheels: Azure, the 400M-seat Office/Copilot base, and the Windows/GitHub/LinkedIn engine. A fortress balance sheet self-funds the AI super-cycle while still compounding, and deep switching costs make the recurring revenue extraordinarily durable.

growth.name: Broadcom
growth.risks: Extreme customer concentration — a single hyperscaler program slipping or being insourced is material.
growth.rationale: The second pillar of AI silicon and the default partner for hyperscalers building a non-NVIDIA option: custom XPUs plus the AI networking fabric, with a path to $100B+ AI revenue by 2027 against a huge backlog. The sticky VMware software base funds dividends and buybacks. Owning both the accelerator design and the network to connect it is a durable two-sided position.

growth.name: Amazon
growth.risks: Heavy AI capex pressuring free cash flow if cloud demand cools.
growth.rationale: Uniquely both the consumer platform (Prime/retail) and a picks-and-shovels layer of the AI boom (AWS), with a third high-margin engine (advertising) compounding fast. The decade story is mix-shift: as high-margin AWS and ads outgrow retail, operating margin and free cash flow compound well above the topline.

growth.name: Meta Platforms
growth.risks: Reality Labs capital sink plus large AI capex with uncertain ROI.
growth.rationale: Owns four of the largest attention platforms on earth and is converting that distribution into an AI advertising machine that lifts ad pricing. Still-undermonetized surfaces (WhatsApp, Reels) plus owned AI silicon give a long runway atop a 40%+ operating-margin core. AI agents and glasses are a free call option on the cash machine.

growth.name: Taiwan Semiconductor
growth.risks: Taiwan geopolitical / China-invasion tail risk that is hard to hedge.
growth.rationale: The indispensable foundry where essentially all leading-edge AI silicon is built, with a near-monopoly on advanced nodes and the CoWoS packaging that is the current AI bottleneck. It wins regardless of which chip designer wins, and process leadership compounds as each node costs more and fewer players can fund it.

growth.name: Eli Lilly
growth.risks: Rich valuation leaves little room for trial/label disappointments or late-decade competition.
growth.rationale: Decisively winning the obesity/GLP-1 race, with an approved oral pill unlocking a global TAM plausibly worth $150B+/yr, plus a deep next-gen pipeline (triple-agonist, Alzheimer's, oncology). Manufacturing scale and pricing power form a wide moat, and it diversifies the portfolio away from the AI-heavy core.

growth.name: Visa
growth.risks: Account-to-account rails (Pix, UPI) and regulatory fee pressure over the long tail.
growth.rationale: The lowest-risk way to own secular digital-payments and emerging-market spend growth: a global toll-road with 50%+ margins, a two-sided network moat, and cash-to-card tailwinds. Heavy buybacks compound EPS at low-to-mid-teens even in mature markets, and it is integrating stablecoin rails as settlement rather than treating them as a threat.

growth.name: Nu Holdings
growth.risks: Heavy reliance on unsecured consumer credit makes earnings sensitive to LatAm credit cycles.
growth.rationale: The world's largest pure digital bank by customers (135M+) with a near-zero-marginal-cost model monetizing financial inclusion across Brazil, Mexico and Colombia. A ~29% ROE proves the model scales profitably, while rising ARPAC and the young Mexico/Colombia books are the decade's second and third growth engines.

growth.name: MercadoLibre
growth.risks: Credit-quality deterioration if LatAm consumers weaken.
growth.rationale: Latin America's dominant commerce-plus-fintech flywheel, with a logistics moat and a payments/lending network throwing off proprietary data across an 800M+ underbanked population. The highest-margin ad and credit segments scaling should drive durable 20-30% growth with eventual margin expansion.

growth.name: GE Vernova
growth.risks: A sharp data-center-capex slowdown would hit order momentum against a high starting valuation.
growth.rationale: The purest large-cap play on the global power supercycle — gas turbines, grid electrification and nuclear SMRs — the physical backbone the AI/data-center load boom depends on. A ~100 GW turbine backlog and record electrification orders, with durable multi-decade tailwinds and a distinct, non-semiconductor angle on AI.

growth.name: ServiceNow
growth.risks: Premium valuation with little room for a stumble; AI-agent pricing may cannibalize seats.
growth.rationale: The system-of-action workflow platform for the enterprise, with ~98% renewal rates and a single-platform architecture where each module makes the others stickier. Its agentic-AI layer is scaling fast on a path toward a $30B revenue goal, turning enterprise AI agents into an expansion vector rather than a threat.

dividend.name: Mastercard
dividend.rationale: The premier capital-light payments compounder: a ~0.7% yield but a ~19% payout and ~14-15%/yr dividend growth (15 straight years) — the longest compounding runway of the two networks. Total return is dividend growth + 60% margins + buybacks, not headline yield.

dividend.name: Visa
dividend.risks: Interchange-fee caps and antitrust pressure on the duopoly.
dividend.rationale: The other half of the global payments toll-booth, with 60%+ margins and a ~0.8% yield that understates the engine: a sub-25% payout and 13-16%/yr dividend hikes plus aggressive buybacks. The worldwide cash-to-digital migration gives a decade-plus runway of double-digit FCF growth.

dividend.name: Microsoft
dividend.rationale: The stickiest enterprise franchise on earth plus #2 cloud and an AI-monetization lead, on a fortress balance sheet. The ~0.9% yield has grown ~10-11%/yr for a decade against a low ~21% payout, leaving ample room to keep raising even while funding record AI capex.

dividend.name: Broadcom
dividend.risks: Hyperscaler customer concentration and an AI-capex digestion cycle.
dividend.rationale: An AI-infrastructure cash machine (FCF ~46% of revenue) with a sticky VMware software arm. The ~0.7% dividend has risen 14 straight years at ~12-13%/yr and the cash ramp lets the payout keep compounding double-digits. Highest-upside total-return name here, with commensurately higher variance.

dividend.name: AbbVie
dividend.risks: US drug-pricing reform plus next-decade Skyrizi/Rinvoq patent exposure.
dividend.rationale: The post-Humira inflection is confirmed, with Skyrizi and Rinvoq driving double-digit growth. The ~3.3-3.5% yield is among the highest in large-cap pharma, backed by a 13-year hike streak with room for mid-single-digit growth — the best high-yield-plus-growth combination in the set, at a reasonable multiple.

dividend.name: S&P Global
dividend.risks: Cyclical debt-issuance volumes hit ratings in recessions; premium multiple.
dividend.rationale: A wide-moat ratings duopoly plus dominant index and data franchises throwing off recurring, capital-light cash flow with heavy pricing power. The low ~24% payout and 50+ year King status support ~7%/yr dividend growth atop double-digit EPS growth and buybacks.

dividend.name: Marsh & McLennan
dividend.risks: Premium valuation re-rating down if organic growth or insurance pricing softens.
dividend.rationale: The world's largest insurance brokerage and risk/HR advisory — an asset-light fee compounder that grows with global premium inflation and risk complexity without bearing underwriting risk. ~1.9% yield with 16 straight years of ~10%/yr hikes and recession-resilient recurring fees.

dividend.name: Linde
dividend.risks: Low starting yield and exposure to the global industrial cycle.
dividend.rationale: The world's largest industrial-gas company with a near-monopoly regional moat: 10-15 year take-or-pay contracts, on-site plants, and contractual inflation pass-through. A 30+ year grower compounding the payout near 8%/yr on 6-9% EPS growth, with a clean-hydrogen/decarbonization backlog layered on.

dividend.name: Enterprise Products Partners
dividend.risks: K-1/UBTI tax complexity and long-run energy-transition demand decline.
dividend.rationale: Gold-standard midstream MLP with toll-like, fee-based cash flows and a fortress balance sheet. Yields ~6.1% with 1.8x distribution coverage and 27 straight years of increases, self-funding growth from retained cash — the anchor income holding (note: issues a K-1).

dividend.name: JPMorgan Chase
dividend.risks: Cyclicality: a deep recession lifts credit losses and curbs capital return.
dividend.rationale: A best-in-class money-center bank with a fortress balance sheet and ~17%+ through-cycle ROTCE. After clearing the 2026 stress test it lifted the dividend ~10% and authorized a fresh $50B buyback, blending a growing yield, double-digit hikes and heavy share-count reduction.
